title: Rules of Engagement and Threat Discrimination
subtitle: Medium | 20–35 min

slides.title: Overview
slides.content: Pulling the trigger at the wrong target is worse than not pulling it at all. Module 3 introduces multi-contact scenarios where one of the targets is a bird — and one is a neutral surveillance drone. Your discipline under pressure is what is being evaluated.

slides.title: Learning Objectives
content: Apply ROE to determine engagement authority
Distinguish between hostile, unknown, and non-engageable contacts
Justify a hold-fire or no-engagement decision
Use the Hold Fire function correctly
Recognize ambient traffic types and classify them correctly

slides.title: Key Concepts
content: Positive ID requirement before engagement — always
Surveillance UAS vs. attack UAS: assessing intent, not just type
CLEAR AIRSPACE function and ATC coordination procedures
When NOT to engage is as important as when to engage
ROE violations: consequences in real-world operations

slides.title: Standards
content: Zero ROE violations — no birds, balloons, or neutral tracks engaged
Correct non-engagement: surveillance contacts identified and not engaged
Hold Fire applied correctly to at least one ambiguous track
Threat discrimination: ≥2/3 hostile tracks correctly identified and defeated
